title: Deriving Call Delta

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-06-finance.md

setup.narrative: European call price (no dividends): c = S * N(d_1) - K * exp(-rT) * N(d_2). Delta = dc/dS.
rules: N(d_1) and N(d_2) ARE functions of S (via d_1 and d_2).
setup.question: Compute Delta = dc/dS.

learningObjectives: Differentiate c carefully treating N(d_1) and N(d_2) as functions of S.
Apply the chain rule to dN(d)/dS.
Use the identity S * N'(d_1) = K * exp(-rT) * N'(d_2) to cancel.

stages.label: Set up the derivative
stages.prompt: dc/dS = N(d_1) + S * dN(d_1)/dS - K * exp(-rT) * dN(d_2)/dS. (Both N(d_i) terms must be differentiated.)  Compute dN(d_i)/dS using the chain rule.

options.text: dN(d_i)/dS = N'(d_i) * dd_i/dS = N'(d_i) / (S * sigma * sqrt(T)). (Both d_1 and d_2 have the same dd/dS since they differ by a constant.)

stages.explanation: d_1 = ln(S/K)/(sigma*sqrt(T)) + (r + sigma^2/2)*sqrt(T)/sigma. dd_1/dS = 1/(S * sigma * sqrt(T)). Same for d_2 (differs by constant -sigma*sqrt(T)). Chain rule: dN(d)/dS = N'(d) * dd/dS.

stages.label: The cancellation identity
stages.prompt: The key identity S * N'(d_1) = K * exp(-rT) * N'(d_2) holds (verifiable from d_1 - d_2 = sigma*sqrt(T) and the explicit form of N'). Use this in the derivative.

options.text: S * dN(d_1)/dS - K * exp(-rT) * dN(d_2)/dS = [S*N'(d_1) - K*exp(-rT)*N'(d_2)] / (S*sigma*sqrt(T)) = 0 / (S*sigma*sqrt(T)) = 0.

stages.explanation: The two derivative terms have a common factor 1/(S*sigma*sqrt(T)). The identity S*N'(d_1) = K*exp(-rT)*N'(d_2) makes the numerator vanish.

stages.label: Conclude Delta = N(d_1)
stages.prompt: After cancellation, what's left of dc/dS?

options.text: Delta = N(d_1).  (The chain-rule terms cancel; only the explicit N(d_1) factor in c remains.)

stages.explanation: After the cancellation, dc/dS = N(d_1). For a call, Delta = N(d_1) -- ALWAYS in (0, 1). With dividends, Delta = exp(-yT) * N(d_1).

stages.label: Commit numerical example
stages.explanation: N(0.1) ≈ 0.5398. So Delta ≈ 0.54 -- slightly above 0.5 for ATM calls (because ITM-finishing probability exceeds 50% under risk-neutral measure when r > 0 or sigma > 0).

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Derive Put Delta. (Hint: from put-call parity differentiate both sides w.r.t. S.)
Why is N(d_1) the 'risk-neutral probability the option finishes in-the-money under the STOCK measure'? Connect to the change-of-numeraire intuition.
What's the analogous formula for digital (cash-or-nothing) options?
